media_title: In the Heat of the Night

characters.name: open/close all folders
characters.media_title: In the Heat of the Night
characters.description: A black detective from Philadelphia. Adaptation Personality Change: Tibbs is a lot more assertive and temperamental in the movie than he was in the book. Feet-First Introduction: Tibbs, disembarking from the train during the opening credits. We don't see his face (or see him again at all) until some ten minutes into the film. Fish Out of Water: Tibbs, the black big-city police detective, making his way in a racist small-town Mississippi backwater. Good Is Not Soft: Tibbs is a good man and extremely dignified, but he absolutely doesn’t take disrespect lying down. When Endicott slaps him, Tibbs hits him in the face even harder.

characters.name: Virgil Tibbs
characters.media_title: In the Heat of the Night
characters.description: A black detective from Philadelphia. Adaptation Personality Change: Tibbs is a lot more assertive and temperamental in the movie than he was in the book. Feet-First Introduction: Tibbs, disembarking from the train during the opening credits. We don't see his face (or see him again at all) until some ten minutes into the film. Fish Out of Water: Tibbs, the black big-city police detective, making his way in a racist small-town Mississippi backwater. Good Is Not Soft: Tibbs is a good man and extremely dignified, but he absolutely doesn’t take disrespect lying down. When Endicott slaps him, Tibbs hits him in the face even harder.

characters.name: Bill Gillespie
characters.media_title: In the Heat of the Night
characters.description: The racist, surly chief of police in Sparta. Big Damn Heroes: When Tibbs is cornered by four racists who plan to lynch him in retaliation for slapping Endicott, Gillespie arrives and runs them off. Break the Haughty: Gillespie starts off the film as a casual racist, and the stereotypical "country hick" chief of police in a small Southern town. After meeting Tibbs, a black homicide investigator who is far more competent at police work than he'll ever be, Gillespie grows more respectful to Virgil, especially after the latter proves his prime suspect to be innocent no less thanthree times(the first of whom was Tibbs himself). By the end, he warmly bids Virgil goodbye at the train station, thanks him for his help, and shakes his hand. Don't You Dare Pity Me!: Tibbs and Gillespie seem to be bonding during a quiet moment, but the moment Tibbs says something remotely sympathetic about Gillespie's lack of romance, Gillespie turns cold and mean on a dime. Expy: Rod Steiger received directions to base his performance as Sheriff Bill Gillespie onJ.W. the Dodge Safety Sheriff, a stereotypical southern sheriff character portrayed by actor Joe Higgins and used as a popular advertisingmascotfor Dodge automobiles in the 1960s. Steiger took the advice, although he greatly toned down the comedic aspects of the character.

characters.name: Sam Wood
characters.media_title: In the Heat of the Night
characters.description: One of the few police officers in Sparta and Gillespie's usual right-hand man, if only by default. The Ditz: Not very smart or competent. The Peeping Tom: Sam stops on his nightly patrol route to gawk at Delores Purdy, who parades around her kitchen in the nude. Red Herring: Sam is suspected to be the killer deposited a large amount of cash around the time of the murder, and lied about the road he drove onduring his beat. The reason for his lie is, he drove that route to eye Delores Purdy, who is notorious for parading around in the nude, and he didn't want Tibbs to see and/or Gillespie to know about it. He also couldn't have murdered Colbert, because the person who killed Colbert drove the dead man's car. Sam couldn't have driven that car and his own patrol car.

characters.name: Eric Endicott
characters.media_title: In the Heat of the Night
characters.description: A wealthy local plantation owner. Adaptational Jerkass: Endicott is a sympathetic character in the novel with no hint of racial prejudice. In the movie he's an unabashed racist, and a few toughs try to gang-beat Tibbs after their confrontation. Establishing Character Moment: Endicott's status as aPolitically Incorrect Villainis established before he even appears onscreen — Tibbs and Gillespie see dozens of Black workers picking cotton as they drive up to his plantation house, which has a statue of a lawn jockey next to the walkway. In person, he seems slightly cordial to Tibbs, but begins talking about hothouse orchids as being "like the Negro", showing his paternalistic "white man's burden" attitude and the contempt he held for Colbert's integrated factory plans. Politically Incorrect Villain: Mr. Endicott's racism stands out even against that of the rest of Sparta,although he's actually not the villain at all. Just a racist jerk.

characters.name: Mrs. Colbert
characters.media_title: In the Heat of the Night
characters.description: Widow of victim Phillip Colbert. Women Are Wiser: She has full faith in Tibbs's skills as a detective, and it's on her insistence that he's retained on the case.

characters.name: Harvey Oberst
characters.media_title: In the Heat of the Night
characters.description: A suspect for Colbert's murder. Because You Were Nice to Me: Oberst starts off as racist and hostile towards Tibbs. After Tibbs proves Harvey is innocent and stops Gillespie from having a confession beaten out of him, Oberst becomes his most vocal supporter.

characters.name: Delores Purdy
characters.media_title: In the Heat of the Night
characters.description: A promiscuous teenager. Dude Magnet: Harvey Oberst, Sam Wood, andRalph Henshaware all attracted to Delores,with the latter having even gotten her pregnant, despite the fact thatshe's only 16. It doesn't help that she parades through her kitchen in the nude every night with her window curtains wide open. Making Love in All the Wrong Places: Delores tells Gillespie that Sam got her pregnant after taking her to the cemetery for sex, although this turns out to be false testimony. (Justified in-universe; in a hot-as-balls place like Sparta, a cool granite slab in a cemetery is a relief.)

characters.name: Lloyd Purdy
characters.media_title: In the Heat of the Night
characters.description: Delores's protective, but deeply racist older brother. Asshole Victim:Ralph murders Lloyd only seconds after he tried to lynch Tibbs in cold blood. Big Brother Instinct: Lloyd is furious when he believes that Sam Wood had sex with his younger sister Delores (who is below the age of consent of Mississippi, making it statutory rape) and got her pregnant, and comes to the police station to press charges.Later, when Tibbs reveals to him that Ralph Henshaw is the real father of her baby, Purdy is so enraged that he forgets all about lynching Tibbs and turns on Ralph with a shotgun for "turn[ing] my little sister into a field slut", prompting Ralph to shoot him in self-defense. Politically Incorrect Villain: Like most of the people in Sparta, Lloyd is a casual racist. He takes it even further than most byorganizing a mob to hunt down Tibbs for the "crime" of being present at Delores's interrogation.

characters.name: Ralph Henshaw
characters.media_title: In the Heat of the Night
characters.description: A clerk and the only employee of the localGreasy Spoondiner. Chekhov's Gunman:Ralph has a number of scenes in which he takes a prominent role in spite of seeming to have nothing to do with the murder. He's the first person we see in the film, shooting a rubber band at a fly. He occasionally taunts Sam by hiding pie and begins one scene by rigging the jukebox to play a song so he can dance by himself. The film justifies all this attention by revealing him to be the murderer. The Dog Was the Mastermind:He's a minor character mostly around for comic relief. He turns out to be the murderer. Jerkass: While no one in Sparta is especially pleasant, Ralph is a dick to everyone. He enjoys screwing with his customers, especially Sam, and is disrespectful to Tibbs out of clear racism.
